title: When Will That Day Come?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. The Tamil section is a source-checked reading copy derived from the OCR and does not alter the canonical source file. Source-page review restored the opening `உடன்பிறப்பே,`, which OCR omitted, and confirmed the terminal date as `7-6-2014`. Sonia Gandhi's English letter is retained as visibly printed, while OCR-confused Latin characters in that quotation are repaired. Other changes are limited to unambiguous OCR word joins. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Today is 7-6-2014!

My memory now travels ten years into the past and comes to rest on this same date, 7 June, in 2004! Yes, it was on 7-6-2004 that Dr A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, President of India, addressing a joint sitting of Parliament in Delhi, announced: “Tamil will be declared as a Classical Language.” That announcement immersed genuine Tamil scholars, lovers of Tamil and Tamils living throughout the world in an ocean of joy.

Following this, at the special conference of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam held in Salem on 21 and 22 August 2004, a resolution was moved by Professor M.P. Balasubramaniam, one of our devoted functionaries who quietly serves the movement, and seconded by our Thiruvilliputhur brother S. Amudhan. The resolution said:

“For the past century, many Tamil scholars have furnished sound evidence and insisted that Tamil is an ancient, unique and classical language of the highest order. Even while serving as Chief Minister, Kalaignar repeatedly pressed Prime Minister Vajpayee, both by letter and in person, to secure the Union government’s recognition of this special distinction. The demand that Tamil be declared a Classical Language was also included in the DMK’s parliamentary election manifesto and pursued continuously. In keeping with these efforts, this conference welcomes the announcement in the President’s Address accepting the demand on behalf of the United Progressive Alliance government at the Centre, and requests that the appropriate government order be issued without delay.”

That was the resolution. The United Progressive Alliance government at the Centre, functioning under the guidance of Sonia Gandhi and the leadership of Dr Manmohan Singh, subsequently issued a notification on 12-10-2004 declaring Tamil a Classical Language.

You who know me completely will surely understand, Udanpirappē, how jubilant I must have been when that notification appeared, how intensely my heart must have leapt with joy, and how deeply pride must have stirred within me. The happiness that then took up residence in me equalled the happiness I felt whenever Thanthai Periyar patted me in appreciation, and whenever Arignar Anna embraced me, kissed my forehead and showered affection upon me.
I think it appropriate to recall here a letter Sonia Gandhi wrote to me at that time:
November 8, 2005
Dear Thiru Karunanidhiji,
I have received your letter of 28th October.

I am glad that all the formalities for declaring Tamil as a Classical Language have now been completed. This is an achievement for all the constituents of the UPA Government, but particular credit goes to you and your Party.
With Regards,
Yours sincerely,
Sonia Gandhi.

The request that Tamil be declared a Classical Language was sent to the Union government many times, not merely during the rule of the United Progressive Alliance, but long before it. Whether in government or functioning as the opposition, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam has always continued to raise its voice tirelessly for the ideals it has embraced. The history of the movement contains countless documentary examples. Securing recognition of Tamil as a Classical Language was one of the movement’s ideals. Continuing the efforts it had made while in office, the movement pursued that ideal without interruption even when it was in opposition.
As President of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, I wrote to Prime Minister Vajpayee as early as 22-4-2003 on the need to declare Tamil a Classical Language. I wrote:

“It is my heartfelt request that Tamil be declared a Classical Language. I once again bring to your attention this demand, which I have pressed for many years. No scholar has yet discovered when Tamil first appeared. Many scholars have said that Tamil is the most ancient language, born before stone appeared and before the earth took shape. That alone proclaims Tamil’s antiquity. I have long urged the Union government to declare such a glorious Tamil a Classical Language. I have appealed to the Union government many times over this demand. I trust that the National Democratic Alliance government under your leadership will now fulfil this long-awaited request.”

In that same year, 2003, Union ministers and Members of Parliament from the DMK, PMK and MDMK met Prime Minister Vajpayee in person and submitted a petition pressing him to declare Tamil a Classical Language. It was the combined force of all these requests that emerged as the Union government’s announcement in 2004.

After this hard-won declaration, I placed a request before my friend Arjun Singh, then Union Minister for Human Resource Development. He accepted it warmly, and as a result we brought the Central Institute of Classical Tamil itself from Mysore to Tamil Nadu. When the necessary order and the Union government’s approval of the institute’s statutory rules were delayed, I sent one of my secretaries, our brother K. Rajamanickam, IAS, to Delhi. Together with our brother T.R. Baalu, then a Union minister, he met Arjun Singh in person. I remember how the Union minister himself took the lead and secured the necessary approvals and orders from the Union government on that very day.

At the inauguration of the Central Institute of Classical Tamil on 30-6-2008, I announced that I would provide a fund of Rs. 1 crore to establish an annual purse of Rs. 10 lakh for a scholar distinguished in Classical Tamil research. Accordingly, on 21-7-2008, I contributed Rs. 1 crore from my own funds to establish the “Kalaignar Karunanidhi Classical Tamil Trust” at the institute.

At the Secretariat in Chennai, in the place where the Assembly complex had stood, we established the Pavendhar Classical Tamil Research Library and brought together there ancient Tamil books and rare palm-leaf manuscripts.

As Chief Minister, I personally visited an important government complex on Kamarajar Salai along Chennai’s seafront that had been assigned to the Classical Tamil institute. I frequently gathered Tamil scholars there and devised plans for the further growth of Classical Tamil. Under the Kazhagam government, land and funding were allotted and every arrangement made for a new building for the Central Institute. What has become of that institution and that declaration, secured through so much effort, since the Kazhagam government changed in 2011?

Because I was principally responsible for bringing about “Classical Tamil,” this AIADMK government, and Chief Minister Jayalalithaa in particular, seems to suffer an allergy at the very mention of it. They appear to regard even thinking about it as a sin. Before they had even taken office, they picked up the Pavendhar Classical Tamil Research Library operating at the Secretariat and threw it out. No one now even knows where that library functions. Nor do we know what has become of the Classical Tamil office, or whether it operates at all.

From funds I received from Sun Television, I gave Rs. 1 crore to the Classical Tamil Research Institute and requested that the income from it be used to confer an annual award of Rs. 10 lakh on a Tamil scholar distinguished in Tamil-language research. Since the AIADMK assumed office, even that award has not been presented properly.

Construction of the building intended for the Central Institute of Classical Tamil has not been expedited and remains stalled. A few days ago, the institute’s vice-chairman, Dr Avvai Natarajan, wrote to the Union Ministry of Human Resource Development saying that the removal of experienced Tamil scholars from the institute and steps to conduct a fresh selection had caused great confusion. Although the Union government appointed Avvai Natarajan vice-chairman, he has reportedly been given no corresponding authority, while full powers have been vested in the director. The present director of IIT Madras is also serving as the institute’s director.

On all these matters, the AIADMK government in Tamil Nadu behaves with careless indifference, as though asking, “What is it to us?” One must doubt whether the Chief Minister or any minister has visited the institute’s office even once during the past three years, or held a single meeting to discuss its work.

Tamil is presently an official language in Tamil Nadu and Puducherry. It is one of the three official languages of Sri Lanka. Tamil is also among the official languages of Singapore and Malaysia. It has constitutional recognition in South Africa. But under the AIADMK government in Tamil Nadu, does Classical Tamil itself enjoy recognition?

Classical Tamil does not belong exclusively to Tamil scholars or to the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam. Everyone who cares for the Tamil language shares responsibility for it. What benefits can Tamil Nadu gain from Tamil’s recognition as a Classical Language?

Independent Tamil departments will be created in universities throughout India. If the University Grants Commission recognises Tamil as a Classical Language, universities across the world will recognise it. The standing of the Tamil language and the Tamil people will rise internationally. Tamil literature will find a place in international encyclopaedias of language. Dedicated chairs for Tamil will be established in universities. Just as the Government of India directly allocates Rs. 40 crore to Sanskrit and another Rs. 100 crore through government-supported institutions to aid its development, opportunities will arise for assistance to the development of Tamil. Tamil literature and modern writing will be translated not only into Indian languages but into English, French and other languages, and spread throughout the world.

Does the AIADMK government act even with these benefits in mind? The painful answer is “No.” Because I was responsible for the declaration recognising Tamil as a Classical Language, this government displays an allergy whenever Classical Tamil is mentioned, without considering the benefits it can bring. Should it not reform itself? Should not all Tamils and everyone with a feeling for Tamil reflect on this?
Today I recall that day when the President announced Tamil’s Classical Language status in Parliament.
With affection,
M.K.
